chapter: JEFP109 – The Book That Saved the Earth
instructions: Read each passage carefully and answer the questions that follow. Answers must be taken only from the passage.

passages.paragraph: The play 'The Book That Saved the Earth' is written by Claire Boiko. It is set in the twenty-fifth century and is narrated by a Historian. The Historian explains that the twentieth century was known as the ‘Era of the Book’ because books were the main source of knowledge during that time.
questions: Who wrote the play 'The Book That Saved the Earth'?
In which century is the play set?
Who narrates the play?
What was the twentieth century called?
Why was the twentieth century called the ‘Era of the Book’?

passages.paragraph: Think-Tank is the ruler of Mars and considers himself extremely intelligent. He is arrogant and often admires himself in the mirror. He believes that Earth is a primitive planet and plans to invade it without understanding its culture.
questions: Who is Think-Tank?
How does Think-Tank view himself?
What habit shows Think-Tank’s arrogance?
How does Think-Tank view Earth?
What plan does Think-Tank make about Earth?

passages.paragraph: Think-Tank sends a group of Martians to Earth led by Captain Omega. The Martians land in a public library. They have no knowledge of books and are confused by what they see around them.
questions: Who leads the Martian mission to Earth?
Where do the Martians land on Earth?
What place do the Martians enter?
Why are the Martians confused?
What do the Martians lack knowledge about?

passages.paragraph: Inside the library, the Martians mistake books for sandwiches. They believe the pages are layers of food. This misunderstanding shows their ignorance about reading and learning.
questions: What do the Martians mistake books for?
Why do the Martians think books are sandwiches?
Where does this misunderstanding take place?
What does this confusion show about the Martians?
Which human skill do the Martians lack?

passages.paragraph: Think-Tank orders the Martians to listen to the books instead of eating them. When nursery rhymes are read aloud, Think-Tank interprets them literally and believes Earth has dangerous powers.
questions: What instruction does Think-Tank give about the books?
What type of poems are read from the books?
How does Think-Tank interpret the nursery rhymes?
What does Think-Tank believe about Earth after listening?
What causes Think-Tank’s fear?

passages.paragraph: The rhyme ‘Hey Diddle Diddle’ frightens Think-Tank because he believes cows can jump over the moon. He thinks Earth animals possess extraordinary abilities.
questions: Which rhyme frightens Think-Tank?
What animal is mentioned in the rhyme?
What does Think-Tank believe the animal can do?
Why does this belief frighten Think-Tank?
What does Think-Tank assume about Earth animals?

passages.paragraph: The rhyme ‘Humpty Dumpty’ makes Think-Tank panic. He believes the rhyme refers to him and predicts his fall from power. This shows his insecurity and fear.
questions: Which rhyme causes Think-Tank to panic?
Whom does Think-Tank think the rhyme refers to?
What does Think-Tank believe the rhyme predicts?
What feeling causes Think-Tank’s panic?
What does this reaction show about Think-Tank?

passages.paragraph: Due to fear created by books, Think-Tank cancels the invasion of Earth. He orders the Martians to leave immediately and flee towards Alpha Centauri.
questions: Why does Think-Tank cancel the invasion?
What decision does Think-Tank take about Earth?
What order does Think-Tank give to the Martians?
Where do the Martians flee?
What causes the Martians to retreat?

passages.paragraph: After Think-Tank flees, Noodle becomes the ruler of Mars. Unlike Think-Tank, Noodle is calm, intelligent, and willing to learn. Earthlings later teach the Martians how to read.
questions: Who becomes the ruler of Mars after Think-Tank?
How is Noodle different from Think-Tank?
What quality makes Noodle a better leader?
What do Earthlings teach the Martians?
What change occurs on Mars after Think-Tank leaves?

passages.paragraph: The play ends with peace between Earth and Mars. It conveys the message that knowledge and books are more powerful than weapons. Education removes fear and prevents conflict.
questions: How does the play end?
What message does the play convey?
What are shown to be more powerful than weapons?
What does education remove?
What does education help prevent?
